summary:
Mikhail Gorbachev's leadership of the Soviet Union from March 1985 to December 1991 inaugurated one of the most consequential political transformations of the twentieth century. A relatively young reformer within the Communist Party gerontocracy, Gorbachev came to power recognizing that the Soviet system was stagnating economically and militarily exhausted by the [[event:2698|Afghan War]]. He introduced two interrelated reform programs: glasnost (openness), which relaxed censorship and allowed public discussion of the system's failures, and perestroika (restructuring), which attempted to decentralize and improve the economy while maintaining Communist Party authority.

Gorbachev dramatically altered Soviet foreign policy, withdrawing from Afghanistan, reducing Soviet forces in Europe, and allowing Eastern European satellite states to determine their own political futures — a renunciation of the Brezhnev Doctrine that had justified Soviet intervention to preserve communist governments. This led to the largely peaceful revolutions of 1989 in which communist regimes fell across Eastern Europe. Gorbachev also pursued substantial arms reduction agreements with the United States, ending the [[event:113|Cold War]] confrontation.

The reforms unleashed forces Gorbachev could not control. Glasnost enabled the airing of nationalist grievances across the multi-ethnic Soviet state, perestroika disrupted economic functioning without replacing it with market alternatives, and the renunciation of coercion as a political tool undermined the foundations of Soviet power. The [[event:3481|coup attempt]] of August 1991 by hardliners, the collapse of Gorbachev's political authority, and the declarations of independence by Soviet republics led to the dissolution of the USSR in December 1991. Gorbachev received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1990 for his role in ending the Cold War.
